content.problemStatement:
Chef is playing a game with his brother. Chef has a sequence of NNN distinct integers (indexed from 111), which he keeps hidden from his brother. Chef's brother should find the index of the second largest integer in this sequence. In order to do that, he may ask any number of questions of the following kind: "Is the iii-th element of the sequence bigger than the jjj-th element?" Chef immediately answers "yes" or "no" to each question.

In order to help his brother, Chef initially gave him MMM pieces of information. Each of them is in the form of an answer to one question: "The AAA-th element of the sequence is bigger than the BBB-th element."

Chef agreed to take his little brother to the cinema if he could ask some questions (possibly zero) in such a way that the index of the second largest element is uniquely determined by them. However, if he asks too many questions, it will be too late and they won't go to the cinema. Since Chef's brother wants to go to the cinema, he wants to ask questions in such a way that the maximum possible number of questions he has to ask is minimised, so that they finish the game quickly. On the other hand, Chef is too lazy to take his brother to the cinema, so he wants the game to last as long as possible; that's why, every time after his brother asks a question, he may permute the sequence in any way such that the resulting sequence is still consistent with all the information his brother has, and only then answer the question.

Find the number of questions Chef's brother has to ask before he can uniquely determine the second largest element of the sequence if both Chef and his brother play optimally.
constraints: There is at least one sequence satisfying all M initial conditions
No two pieces of information will be about the same pair of elements
The sum of N for all test cases does not exceed 300000
The sum of M for all test cases does not exceed 300000
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The description of T test cases follows. The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers N and M. M lines follow. Each of these lines contains two space-separated integers A and B, meaning that the A-th element of the sequence is bigger than the B-th element.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer — the number of questions Chef's brother has to ask in order to know the second largest element.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The sequence can be [1, 2, 3, 4] or [1, 2, 4, 3]. To determine the second largest element, we need to ask at least 2 questions.
The sequence can be [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] or [1, 2, 4, 3, 5]. To determine the second largest element, we need to ask at least 2 questions.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a graph-based approach. We create a directed graph where each node represents an element in the sequence. We add a directed edge from node A to node B if A is bigger than B. Then, we perform a topological sorting on the graph to find the largest element. After that, we remove the largest element from the graph and perform another topological sorting to find the second largest element. The number of questions Chef's brother has to ask is equal to the number of nodes that have the same in-degree as the second largest element.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * (N + M)) — where T is the number of test cases, N is the number of elements in the sequence, and M is the number of initial conditions.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N + M) — where N is the number of elements in the sequence, and M is the number of initial conditions.
